title: 2023 Disability Survey: Feedback from public consultation October/November 2021 | Stats NZ
content:
## 2023 Disability Survey: Feedback from public consultation October/November 2021
23 May 2022, 10:45am
This page summarises feedback from the 2023 Disability Survey consultation, by topic.
- About the consultation and Disability Survey
- Objectives of the 2023 Disability Survey
- About the Disability Survey
- 2013 Disability Survey
- Te ao Māori perspectives on disability
- Household and general help

## About the consultation and Disability Survey
Here is a summary of feedback, by topic, we received from the 2023 Disability Survey consultation. We are using this feedback to prioritise data needs, in line with the survey objectives.

### Objectives of the 2023 Disability Survey
The objectives of the 2023 Disability Survey were outlined in the consultation process. In priority order, the objectives are:
- to understand the size of the disabled population usually resident in New Zealand and describe characteristics of that population. More specifically to:
- produce reliable national estimates of the disabled population by demographic characteristics, such as age group, gender, and ethnicity
- produce reliable national estimates of the disabled population by functional domain
- to understand the extent to which social, wellbeing, and economic outcomes for disabled people differ from those for non-disabled people, and how those outcomes differ between groups within the disabled population
- to understand the level and type of support disabled people need to perform activities of daily living, including identifying unmet need for support
- to understand what creates barriers or enables participation by disabled people in important aspects of life, such as work, education, civic society, and recreation
Disability Survey 2023: Consultation was open from Monday 6 September to Friday 29 October 2021.
We received feedback from over 200 individuals and organisations across the country, including:
- family/whānau of disabled people
- disabled people’s organisations
- district health boards
- disability support and service organisations
- local councils, researchers, and health and disability workers.
We will publish final decisions on content later this year.

### About the Disability Survey
Stats NZ has held the Disability Survey every 5–10 years since 1996. The understanding of disability within advocacy groups and wider New Zealand has changed greatly over this time. As society shifts, so does the interest in the kinds of data that the Disability Survey collects. When preparing to run the Disability Survey, we consult with stakeholders and review the content to make sure it will meet current data needs.
Our consultation in 2021 aimed to inform the content of the 2023 Disability Survey and create a survey that is more aligned with the needs of disabled people, data users, and other stakeholders.
The Disability Survey is the primary source for estimating disability prevalence and disability sub-group prevalence in Aotearoa New Zealand. It collects information on the lives of disabled and non-disabled people to allow comparisons between the two groups.

### 2013 Disability Survey
In 2013, the Disability Survey consisted of two surveys: the Household Disability Survey and the Disability Survey of Residential Facilities. The Household Disability Survey is a post-censal survey that draws its sample population from Census respondents. This means the 2023 Disability Survey will be carried out shortly after the 2023 Census.
In 2013, the Household Disability Survey had a sample of about 23,000 people, including adults, children, disabled people, and non-disabled people. Responses were collected over the phone where possible. Disabled respondents were asked additional questions (compared to non-disabled respondents) in order to collect more detailed information on disability specific topics.
Children were asked different questions compared to adults for some topics, to ensure questions were age appropriate. Children were defined as those under 15 years of age and their responses were collected by proxy from a parent or caregiver.
The 2013 Disability Survey of Residential Facilities included 200 facilities, and up to five residents from each facility were selected to complete the survey. This survey was restricted to adults and was a shorter questionnaire than the adult Household Disability Survey. Adult respondents in both surveys answered primarily for themselves but had the option of answering by proxy if necessary.
The most common suggestions for new functional domains fit into the following categories:
Additionally, some feedback requested more detailed information on specific and rare impairments.
Feedback asked for a reduction in the use of proxies. It was highlighted that where possible, the survey should be designed for all disabled respondents, including children, to answer the questionnaire for themselves.
In situations where a respondent is unable to complete the survey independently, it was recommended that, rather than defaulting to a proxy interview, we should endeavour to include the respondent in the survey by having a trusted support person present to assist them.
Some feedback suggested that respondents with mild impairments should be asked all the questions that disabled respondents are asked.
We also received feedback rejecting the category of mild impairment, saying that this group should be recorded as disabled.
Some feedback wanted specific information on how mild impairments affect respondents. There was also interest in how the experience of intermittent and short-term impairments varied over time and in different settings.
The Disability Survey classifies respondents as disabled based on their answers to a series of questions about basic functioning. Respondents are asked whether they have any difficulties carrying out a range of functional activities, such as moving, hearing, or seeing.
Respondents answer the functioning questions using this scale:
Respondents must indicate they have at least “ a lot of difficulty ” on this scale to be classified as disabled in the survey. This means some respondents will report “ some difficulty ” with functional activities but will not meet the threshold to be classified as disabled. We refer to this area between unimpaired functioning and disability as a “ mild impairment ”.
Respondents who report a mild impairment will not be considered disabled within the survey but may still face difficulties in some situations.

#### Education and employment
Feedback on education requested information on the difference in access to teacher aide support for students with mild impairments, as well as education outcomes and attendance rates.
Feedback on employment included interest in the experience of discrimination, the number of hours worked, and the type of jobs that are available to respondents with mild impairments.

#### Wellbeing and support
Wellbeing feedback, for people with mild impairments, requested information on mental health, ability to live independently, feeling of choice, feelings of control, and social wellbeing.
Examples of social wellbeing were the:
- ability to communicate effectively
- presence of meaningful social connections
- ability to reach out for support
- experience of discrimination
- ability to be involved within their community.
Some feedback about mild impairments expressed interest in whether respondents experienced discrimination in healthcare, received financial support, and whether the support services understood the respondents’ condition.

#### Disability Survey of Residential Facilities
Throughout the consultation questions, we received feedback indicating a desire for the Disability Survey of Residential Facilities to include the full set of questions that is used in the Household Disability Survey, or at least increase the number of questions asked in residential facilities.
There was strong feedback suggesting that an increased sample size was needed to accurately capture the disabled population. Feedback suggested that this increase would allow for more detailed exploration into the outcomes of disabled youth and Māori disabled people.

### Topics for non-disabled respondents
Feedback about the information we collect on non-disabled respondents, for comparison purposes, mostly reflected agreement with our current approach.
Those who did give feedback about suggested changes had different opinions.
Some feedback suggested all questions should be asked of non-disabled respondents. Some of these suggestions were grounded in the idea that there could be disabled people who had been mistaken as non-disabled in the screening questions. Others suggested all questions should be asked of non-disabled respondents so that thorough comparison could be made with disabled respondents.
Most feedback acknowledged there is no need to ask the non-disabled population questions on access to disability household help, assistive equipment, and carers.
Feedback on healthcare topics for non-disabled respondents focused on collecting information to compare experiences of discrimination, access, and information availability.
Wellbeing information for non-disabled respondents was requested to compare experience of depression, anxiety, trauma, safety, and general wellbeing.
Feedback about social wellbeing topics for non-disabled respondents focused on engagement with social groups, friends, family, community, feelings of loneliness, and participation in leisure activities.
Education and employment feedback focused on the non-disabled respondents’ level of education, and experiences within the workplace, or applying for work.
Feedback on transport experiences for non-disabled respondents focused on availability of public and private modes of transport and the accessibility of destinations.
Housing feedback focused on availability of suitable housing. This includes the ability to choose the location and size of a home, and whether the current home was suitable for the respondent and their household. There was also interest in comparing the number of disabled and non-disabled people who rent or own their home.

## Te ao Māori perspectives on disability
Feedback about how the survey could reflect te ao Māori perspectives on disability reflected the following four themes.

### Tāngata whaikaha Māori and whānau hauā
Tāngata whaikaha Māori is a term used to describe Māori disabled people. The word whaikaha characterises the disabled person as determined to do well, thus bringing an association with strength rather than deficit. Feedback highlighted that understanding this perspective is key to creating a survey that is more responsive to the needs of Māori.
Mana whaikaha korero has more information about this disability support system.
Another term that is used in the disability sector is whānau hauā. Whānau hauā reinforces a collective worldview and whānau centric ways of recognising the needs of a disabled person who is interconnected with their whānau, whakapapa, marae tīpuna, and environment.
Whānau hauā: Reframing disability from an Indigenous perspective presents the alternative approach.
Because of this view of collective wellbeing, feedback showed a strong emphasis on the need to ask questions about whānau hauora, which includes the wellbeing of the broader whānau involved in the disabled person’s life.

### Health and wellbeing
Access to mātauranga Māori informed services were of key interest in feedback. This includes understanding needs for kaupapa Māori services, Rongoā Māori, tamariki ora providers, and Māori health professionals.
Some feedback requested information about differences in access to care, and whether there was exclusion from health and wellbeing services due to intergenerational trauma and ongoing impacts of colonisation.
Interest in wellbeing included measuring community inclusion, engagement with Māori culture, spiritual wellbeing, the health of whānau, and culturally appropriate housing.
Te Whare Tapa Whā, a well-established model of Māori wellbeing, describes four aspects:
- taha tinana (physical wellbeing and health)
- taha wairua (spiritual wellbeing and personal beliefs)
- taha whānau (social wellbeing and self-esteem)
- taha hinengaro (mental and emotional wellbeing, and self-confidence).
Community inclusion was described in some feedback as particularly important because many of the impacts of having an impairment could be mitigated or minimised through having an ability appropriate role within the community.
There was interest in measuring discrimination faced by Māori disabled people, particularly in health settings. This includes discrimination about ethnicity, disability, age, gender, economic status, sexuality, and their intersections.

### General survey approach
Feedback suggested changes to methodology, survey design, and collection. Some feedback showed concern that the Disability Survey questions and content development were framed by an individualistic, Pākeha-centric world view. Feedback emphasised the need to apply a te ao Māori lens to collection and analysis, as well as for Māori data governance mechanisms to protect data. This includes seeking feedback from iwi leaders when questions are being finalised.
One suggestion was that it is necessary to communicate with kaumātua and marae when choosing a sample population. Some feedback suggested that we seek advice from kaupapa Māori organisations for language, concepts, and to ensure our purpose responds to the needs of Māori disabled people. It was also suggested that survey collection needs to be done in a way that allows Māori to assist Māori in answering survey questions.
Some feedback suggested that open answer questions or face-to-face interviews that allow for story telling would be more appropriate for understanding respondent experiences, rather than the closed question formats usually used in surveys for population prevalence. Respondent experiences include more accurate information on self-identification, experience of discrimination, whānau hauora, and differences in experiences when seeking treatment through ACC compared with DHBs (district health boards).
Youth were identified in feedback as an important group to collect data on. During the transition from child to adult, youth experience unique changes in life structure and support. Feedback showed particular interest in youth mental health, education, and employment outcomes.
Young children and older adults were also of key interest in the feedback we received. A common request was for a split in age brackets to reflect school age, tertiary age/young adults, working age, and retirement. However, there were many different ideas about what ages should be included within a ‘youth’ category.
The consultation asked whether people aged 15–17-years should have the same questionnaire as adult respondents, as was done in 2013, or have the same questionnaire as child respondents.
Support for 15–17-year-olds completing the adult question set centered around the idea that they are old enough to answer the questions themselves, and there are additional questions in the adult set that are relevant to their lives. To include 15–17-year-olds in the adult question set aligns with the Census, the Ministry of Health’s treatment guidelines, and pediatric health definitions.
Suggestions to give this age group the same questionnaire as child respondents were largely rooted in a desire to keep the framing consistent with the legal definition of a child. This feedback often referred to the rights and protections of the child.
Although not explicitly asked, some feedback suggested that 15–17-year-olds should be part of a new category for youth. A youth category could better reflect their specific needs and experiences. Some feedback described support systems that were specifically designed for young people and extended to age 24. A youth category could balance the need for people aged 15–17 to answer the questions themselves, whilst avoiding the comparison with young children or older adults.

### Ethnicity and identity
Some feedback suggested collecting ethnicity information for all ethnic groups, in addition to Māori and European. The most requested ethnicities were Pacific and Asian peoples.
We received a few suggestions to collect information about other identity groups such as population groups with refugee or migrant backgrounds, and diverse genders and sexualities.
Most feedback about wellbeing supported the existing measures for mental health, safety, and discrimination. The feedback about additional needs for information are summarised below.
A common theme was the need for information on social contact, inclusion, and community. This included:
- frequency of contact with others
- sense of belonging to a community or neighbourhood
- social and support networks
- ease of finding support
- involvement in clubs or groups.
Feedback also asked for information on whether respondents felt they could be themselves, whether the current level of social interaction was sufficient to meet their needs, and who in their community they feel closest to, or spend the most time with.
Feedback showed interest in broadening the discrimination topic to include harassment, bullying, avoidance, exclusion, negative attitudes, stigma, and prejudice.
There was also interest in the reason for discrimination, whether it was due to disability, age, gender, sexuality, ethnicity, or socio-economic status.
Feedback also indicated an interest in the settings that discrimination occurred, including employment, housing, health, education, or government and other services.

### Subjective wellbeing
Other feedback requested information on sense of purpose, optimism for the future, family wellbeing, and financial wellbeing. Feedback also suggested the inclusion of wellbeing measures of importance to Māori, such as spiritual and cultural wellbeing, and connection to whānau and whakapapa.

### Mental health access
Feedback focusing on access to mental health professionals and services is covered in more detail in the health services section below.

### Ease of finding suitable housing
Feedback requested information on housing-related accessibility requirements. This included data on ease of finding suitable and accessible housing, including whether disabled people have had to turn down housing, and whether they feel limited in their housing choices. Limits include compromising on location, size, or type of home, to meet accessibility requirements.
Discrimination while searching for housing was another interest area discussed in feedback. Examples mentioned in feedback were whether landlords were willing to make accessibility modifications to rental properties, wait times for modifications, and wait times for social housing.

### Suitability of current home
There was feedback asking about the suitability of current housing to meet the needs of disabled respondents. This includes a wide range of factors such as safety, appropriate storage for assistive equipment or medical supplies, adequate space to easily move around, and appropriate building and safety modifications like handrails or widened doorways.
Feedback also identified a need for other housing information, such as whether the home is warm and dry, whether the property is in good condition and easy to maintain, and whether housing is affordable.
There was strong interest in data on tenure (whether people own or rent their home) and tenure security. Tenure security encompasses levels of residential mobility (how often people move house) and other areas of security, such as the amount of control someone has over their housing, and the degree of certainty they have about their future housing circumstances.
There was particular interest in data on tenure security for renters, and on experiences of eviction, insecure housing, homelessness, or emergency housing.

### Neighbourhood and location
As well as the suitability of a home itself, feedback identified a need for information on the home’s location and wider neighbourhood. This includes ease of access to key amenities, green spaces, and appropriate transport; neighbourhood safety; and proximity to workplaces, schools, family, or friends.

## Household and general help
We received several suggestions to add items to the existing list of everyday tasks that a respondent might need assistance with. Some suggestions were: managing money, daily medical needs or accessing healthcare, social support, transport, and recreational activities. The inclusion of recreational and social activities was suggested for both the adult and child questionnaire.
Feedback suggested that expanding the list of tasks would help reflect the varied and diverse support needs of disabled people.
Some feedback called for data on who is providing funding to support, help, and care for disabled people. This includes whether disabled people can access publicly funded in-home support services.
In cases where the respondent was ineligible or unable to access government-funded support services, there was a need to understand where support was coming from. Feedback described disabled people having to rely heavily on family (including parents, partners, children, siblings, and others) and friends.
For respondents with access to support services, information is needed to understand if services were adequate, consistent, and high quality.
There was a particular interest in discrepancies in the level of support provided by ACC versus the Ministry of Health and DHBs.

### Barriers (or difficulties) when accessing support
There was a strong interest in the barriers to accessing disability support, particularly government funded support. Some of the barriers mentioned were cost, lack of information, complicated approval process, long wait times, and strict eligibility criteria.
Feedback described how even when support is provided, it is often not sufficient to meet the disabled person’s needs. This includes the disabled person requiring more hours of support than they are currently receiving or are funded to receive. It also includes receiving support for a specific task, like household chores, but no support for another task they need assistance with, like paperwork, or transport to healthcare appointments.
Feedback also discussed inadequacy of care, including the disabled person having either no choice, or no trust, in the carer they are provided. There were also examples of carers not having adequate training or skills, and a lack of consistency, with disabled people seeing many different support people, instead of one trusted carer.

### Additional health services
Feedback suggested expanding the list of health services in the survey to better capture all types of health care sought by respondents.
The most frequently suggested health services were:
- hospital-based care (including emergency departments)
- mental health services
- allied health professionals (occupational therapists, physiotherapists, speech/language therapists, rehabilitation services)
- Māori health professionals
- paediatricians or child development specialists.
There was also criticism that the existing option of ‘alternative healthcare worker’ was outdated and too vague to be useful.
Feedback requesting information about unmet needs for health services was in line with the 2013 topic on unmet needs.
Feedback requested information on the barriers to accessing healthcare. Describing barriers allows respondents to name the difficulties they face, or have faced, even if they currently have access to a service. The most common barriers suggested were affordability, timeliness, quality, and accessibility. There was also interest in measuring how satisfied respondents were with their current providers approach to health care and level of knowledge about their condition(s).
The section on ‘reason for unmet need’ in the 2013 survey was criticised as too focused on the individual’s limitation, rather than the gaps in service provision.

### Include assistive technologies
Feedback consistently referenced the use of assistive equipment and technology . Examples of assistive technology included augmentative and alternative communication devices, screen readers, smart phones, and specific software.
‘Assistive equipment’ primarily refers to physical or manual equipment such as wheelchairs and crutches. Widening the survey to ask about assistive equipment and technology is not just a change in terminology. It is also indicative of the progress accessible technology has made since 2013, the ever-increasing range of technology products available, and how much more common technology products have become.

### Include all disabled people
Feedback recommended opening assistive equipment questions to all disabled respondents. It was regarded as especially important to include assistive equipment used by the neurodiverse population. In the 2013 Disability Survey, assistive equipment questions were only asked of respondents with impairments relating to hearing, speaking, vision, mobility, or agility.

### Barriers/difficulties accessing assistive equipment and technology
Feedback requested information on access to assistive equipment and technology, including cost, funding, choice, wait time, ease of the application process, and availability of information.
Feedback also asked for the specific types of equipment and technology used, and if it was fit for purpose, needed to be upgraded, or needed to be maintained.
There was also feedback asking for information on how common it was for disabled people to receive government funded assistive equipment and whether experiences differed depending on which government organisation provided the equipment.
Feedback suggested that there are several difficulties faced by disabled children during education. These relate to learning, socialising, behaviour, sensory needs, and physical needs.
It was suggested that the kind of support available can be the difference between a child being able to attend school full time or not.

### Early childhood education
Feedback about early childhood education included the need for information on the availability of services, and barriers to attending. There was also a request for information on the use of early intervention or child development services for pre-school children.

### School attendance and interruptions
Feedback suggested we ask if disabled children attended full-time education, and if not, then the reasons why. Possible reasons for not attending full-time education were health conditions, insufficient support, difficulty enrolling at a suitable school, or exclusions, stand downs and suspensions. There was also interest in how much school was missed in the last 12 months.

### Additional learning support
The focus of feedback about learning support was on the availability and need for support from teacher aides – including the frequency of teacher aide support, how it was funded and whether this level of support is adequate.
Feedback requested information on the use of assistive technology, including sports equipment, and the availability of learning material in accessible formats. There was also a handful of requests for information on various specific funding areas within education.
Previously, the survey included a question on whether children attended special education classes. Feedback considered this term outdated, and no longer relevant.

### Post-school transitions and tertiary study
Feedback requested information on whether disabled respondents received adequate support and information to help with transitioning between secondary school and work or tertiary education.
Feedback also asked for data on tertiary education participation, barriers, and support for disabled respondents. Suggested barriers included the accessibility of content and facilities, available support, affordability, transport, and the availability of suitable courses.
There were also suggestions that unmet needs should be measured and that the questions should be opened to all adults, including respondents aged 65 years and over.

### Limitations and accommodations for employed disabled people
Feedback supported the expansion of questions on workplace accommodations to include flexible working arrangements and non-physical modifications. Flexible work arrangements can include flexibility in the tasks, hours, or location of the respondent’s work. There was interest in whether flexibility is available within the respondent’s workplace, or if there was an unmet need for flexibility.
Other non-physical accommodations could include communication tools, accessibility software, or personal support. There was also interest in whether accommodations have been requested and delivered, and if not, why not.

### Limitations and barriers for non-employed disabled people
Feedback asked for questions about why some respondents have not looked for work or have found it difficult to look for work. There was also interest in what would make it more likely for respondents to seek or find work.
Another type of barrier was discrimination and attitudes of employers and co-workers. There was interest in finding out disabled and non-disabled peoples’ perception of attitudes or discrimination toward disabled people in employment.
The 2013 Disability Survey focused on the limitations of respondents rather than the barriers that limit employment opportunities, and accommodations which might enhance them. Questions on barriers also focused on physical modifications rather than support, training, and flexible work options.
Feedback suggested that volunteer work should be in the employment module because it is not a leisure activity, and it gives non-employed people an opportunity to report that they work despite not being in paid employment.
Feedback also asked for questions about respondents’ reasons for doing volunteer work. Suggested reasons included to get work experience or training, to be actively engaged in the community, or because the respondent was unable to get paid work.

### Earnings and financial wellbeing
Feedback described a need for information on income adequacy and how it relates to wellbeing. Income adequacy does not just relate to employed respondents, and could refer to benefit levels, employment, or ACC payments.
Feedback suggested a need for information on whether there are adequate transport options for disabled people to get where they want to go. Locations could include places for leisure and social activities, as well as accessing necessities such as pharmacies, supermarkets, and doctors.
A key theme in the feedback was the need to ask about accessible journeys, not just the method of transport. While transport is still crucial, other factors should be included, such as access upon arrival at the destination. Access can depend on aspects of the built environment like footpaths, ramps, and road crossings.
Other barriers can include cost, limited options, safety concerns, difficulty accessing information for journey planning, discrimination, and negative attitudes from other passengers or staff.
The availability of accessible journeys can impact the opportunities available to disabled people. Availability of transport can prevent access to employment and study. Disabled people can be forced to change plans or miss appointments due to the inadequacy of transport options. This limits independence, increases isolation, and requires reliance on others.
Active transport (such as walking, cycling, or using a manual wheelchair) was not included in the 2013 survey and feedback identified this as an important data gap for the 2023 survey to address. This could also include low-powered transport options such as e-bikes, mobility scooters, or e-scooters.

### Barriers specific to driving
Feedback indicated a need for more information on barriers specific to driving. This includes barriers such as lack of accessible parking, or difficulties accessing a modified vehicle, such as limited availability or lack of funding.
The suggested changes to the types of leisure activities, can be broadly grouped into:
- entertainment events
Other suggestions were to include an open answer option, and to extend the time frame beyond “ in the last four weeks ”.
Feedback also suggested collecting information on who leisure activities were done with – both in terms of the relationship to the respondent and whether they had an impairment.
Feedback suggested that the child survey answer options should be the same as the adult survey.
Like other topic areas, there were many requests to collect information about barriers to engaging in leisure activities. Suggested barriers included cost, transport, physical accessibility, time, accessible information, and need for support or assistive equipment.
Some feedback asked for information about family carers, such as parents, caregivers, and wider family/whānau supporting disabled children.
Feedback suggested a need for information on the financial situation of families with a disabled child. This included adequacy of income to meet every-day needs – including accommodation, food, and clothing. There was also interest in whether main carers of disabled children were receiving payments as caregivers.
Feedback identified a need for information on any changes to employment in order to accommodate caring for their disabled child. This includes reduced hours, changing jobs, and retraining.
For carers who are not employed, feedback focused on information about whether they left previous employment to care for their disabled child, and whether their caring responsibilities prevent them from working. There was interest in whether the main carer would like to work, and whether they feel they can work and still care for their child.

### Carers of disabled adults
Feedback requested information on the relationship between carers and disabled adults, including the prevalence of young people or children in caring roles.

### Support available to carers
Feedback requested more information on the support available for the carers of disabled people (both children and adults), and whether there was any government assistance. This includes information on access to support systems, including informal support such as friends and relatives, and funded disability support staff.
There was also interest in how well supported carers of disabled children and adults feel, including the availability and ease of accessing of support if the carer needs a break.
Another suggestion was to collect information about the main carers’ stress levels, social impact, mental wellbeing, and satisfaction with leisure time.
We would like to thank all those who provided feedback on the survey content and look forward to continued engagement with you as we work towards a new and improved Disability Survey.
Contact Katy Auberson at DisabilitySurvey2023@stats.govt.nz if you have any questions.
ISBN 978-1-99-003293-6
- Disability Survey 2023: Consultation 06 September 2021
